elements.sent-2411-001: Optimization problems, arise in many practical applications, from the view points of both theory and numerical methods.
elements.sent-2411-002: Especially, significant improvement in deep learning training came from the Quasi-Newton methods.
elements.sent-2411-003: Quasi-Newton search directions provide an attractive alternative to Newton's method in that they do not require computation of the Hessian and yet still attain a superlinear rate of convergence.
elements.sent-2411-004: In Quasi-Newton method, we require Hessian approximation to satisfy the secant equation.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this paper, the Classical Cauchy-Schwartz Inequality is introduced, then more generalization are proposed.
elements.sent-2411-006: And it is seriously proved that Quasi-Newton method is a steepest descent method under the ellipsoid norm.
elements.sent-2411-007: Optimization problems, arise in many practical applications.
elements.sent-2411-008: Especially, signiﬁcant improvement in deep learning training came from the Quasi-Newton methods.
elements.sent-2411-009: The steepest descent method is one of the simplest and the most fundamental minimization methods for unconstrained optimization.
elements.sent-2411-010: The method is a line search method that moves along $ - \nabla f _k $ at every step.
elements.sent-2411-011: One advantage of the steepest descent direction is that it requires calculation of the gradient but not of second derivatives.
elements.sent-2411-012: However, it can be excruciatingly slow on difﬁcult problems.
elements.sent-2411-013: Quasi-Newton methods, like steepest descent, require only the gradient of the objective function to be supplied at each iteration.
elements.sent-2411-014: Classical Cauchy-Schwartz Inequality: if x and y are vectors in $ R ^n $, then $ \left| x ^T y \right| \le \left| \left| x \right| \right| _2 \left| \left| y \right| \right| _2 $ , the equality holds if and only if x and y are linearly dependent.
elements.sent-2411-015: Let $ f \left( x \right) = \left( x - 1 \right) - ln \left( x \right) $, we have $ f \left( 1 \right) = 0, f' \left( x \right) = 1 - \frac {1 {x $.}}

elements.sent-2411-017: Let \begin{equation}R = \sum _{i = 1} ^n \delta _i a _i\end{equation} It follows immediately from the lemma 1 that \begin{equation}\forall i, \frac {a _i} {R} - 1 - ln \left( \frac {a _i } {R} \right) \ge 0\end{equation} Multiplying every item by $ \delta _i $, and taking the sum over $ i $ yields: \begin{equation}\sum _{i=1} ^n \left( \frac {\delta _i a _i} {R} - \delta _i \right) - \sum _{i = 1} ^n \delta _i ln \left( \frac {a _i} {R} \right) \ge 0\end{equation} It follows from (2.1) that $ \sum _{i = 1 ^n \frac {\delta _i a _i {R = 1 $, and with known conditions we obtain $ \sum _{i = 1 ^n \delta _i = 1 $.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Using (2.3), we have : \begin{equation}\sum _{i = 1} ^n \delta _i ln \left( \frac {a _i} {R} \right) \le 0\end{equation} \begin{equation}exp \left[ \sum _{i = 1} ^n \delta _i ln \left( \frac {a _i} {R} \right) \right] \le exp \left( 0 \right) = 1\end{equation} \begin{equation}exp \left[ \sum _{i = 1} ^n ln \left( \frac {a _i ^{\delta _i} } {R ^{\delta _i} } \right) \right] \le 1\end{equation} \begin{equation}exp \left[ ln \left( \frac {a _1 ^{\delta _1} \cdots a _n ^{\delta _n}} {R ^{\delta _1} \cdots R ^{\delta _n}} \right) \right] \le 1\end{equation} Therefore, \begin{equation}\frac {a _1 ^{\delta _1} \cdots a _n ^{\delta _n}} {R} \le 1\end{equation} .
elements.sent-2411-019: So, \begin{equation}a _1 ^{\delta _1} \cdots a _n ^{\delta _n} \le \delta _1 a _1 + \cdots + \delta _n a _n\end{equation} The equality of (2.5) holds if and only if equality of (2.4) holds.
elements.sent-2411-020: When $ a _1 = \cdots = a _n $, $ R = a _1 $, $ \forall i, \frac {a _i {R = 1 $, where equality of (2.4) holds.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: The Arithmetic Mean–Geometric Mean Inequality follows from Lemma 2 by setting $ \delta _1 = \cdots = \delta _n = \frac {1 {n $.}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Furthermore, taking $ n=2 $, we have "Geometric mean not greater than arithmetic mean", which can be found in elementary mathematics.
elements.sent-2411-023: If x and y are also real numbers, then: $ x y \le \frac {x ^p p + \frac {y ^q q $, and equality holds if and only if $ x ^p = y ^q $.}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Setting $ \delta _1 = \frac 1 p, \delta _2 = \frac 1 q $ in the Weighted Arithmetic Mean–Geometric Mean Inequality, we obtain $ \delta _1 + \delta _2 = 1 $.
elements.sent-2411-025: Further setting $ a _1 = x ^p, a _2 = y ^q $, we have \begin{equation}x y = a _1 ^{\frac 1 p} a _2 ^{\frac 1 q} \le \frac {a _1} p + \frac {a _2} q = \frac {x ^p} p + \frac {y ^q} q\end{equation} The equality holds if and only if $ a _1 = a _2 $, that is to say, $ x ^p = y ^q .. $.

elements.sent-2411-027: We will make two generalizations about Cauchy-Schwartz Inequality.
elements.sent-2411-028: One generalization takes account of power, the other one takes vector norm.
elements.sent-2411-029: If $ x = 0 $ or $ y = 0 $, the result is obviously true.
elements.sent-2411-030: We assume that both x and y are not zero.
elements.sent-2411-031: When $ i \in 1, \cdots, n $, from Young inequality, we have: \begin{equation}\frac {\left| x _i y _i \right|} {\left| \left| x \right| \right| _p \left| \left| y \right| \right| _q} = \frac {\left| x _i \right|} {\left| \left| x \right| \right| _p} \frac {\left| y _i \right|} {\left| \left| y \right| \right| _q} \le \frac 1 p \frac {\left| x _i \right| ^p} {\left| \left| x \right| \right| _p ^p} + \frac 1 q \frac {\left| y _i \right| ^q} {\left| \left| y \right| \right| _q ^q}\end{equation} Taking the sum over i on both sides of the above inequality yields \begin{equation}\frac 1 {\left| \left| x \right| \right| _p \left| \left| y \right| \right| _q} \sum _{i = 1} ^n \left| x _i y _i \right| \le \frac 1 {p \left| \left| x \right| \right| _p ^p} \sum _{i = 1} ^n \left| x _i \right| ^p + \frac 1 {q \left| \left| y \right| \right| _q ^q} \sum _{i = 1} ^n \left| y _i \right| ^q = \frac 1 p + \frac 1 q = 1\end{equation} Multiplying $ \left| \left| x \right| \right| _p \left| \left| y \right| \right| _q $ on both sides of (2.7), we obtain the result.
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}\frac {\left| x _i y _i \right|} {\left| \left| x \right| \right| _p \left| \left| y \right| \right| _q} = \frac {\left| x _i \right|} {\left| \left| x \right| \right| _p} \frac {\left| y _i \right|} {\left| \left| y \right| \right| _q} \le \frac 1 p \frac {\left| x _i \right| ^p} {\left| \left| x \right| \right| _p ^p} + \frac 1 q \frac {\left| y _i \right| ^q} {\left| \left| y \right| \right| _q ^q}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-012: \begin{equation}\frac 1 {\left| \left| x \right| \right| _p \left| \left| y \right| \right| _q} \sum _{i = 1} ^n \left| x _i y _i \right| \le \frac 1 {p \left| \left| x \right| \right| _p ^p} \sum _{i = 1} ^n \left| x _i \right| ^p + \frac 1 {q \left| \left| y \right| \right| _q ^q} \sum _{i = 1} ^n \left| y _i \right| ^q = \frac 1 p + \frac 1 q = 1\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-032: If A has singular value decomposition $ U \Sigma V ^T $, then $ A ^T $ has singular value decomposition $ V \Sigma ^T U ^T $.
elements.sent-2411-033: The matrices $ \Sigma $ and $ \Sigma ^T $ will have the same nonzero diagonal elements.
elements.sent-2411-034: Thus $ A $ and $ A ^T $ have the same nonzero singular values.
elements.sent-2411-035: You will find it rather difficult to prove Generalized Cauchy-Schwartz Inequality.
elements.sent-2411-036: In fact it was introduced without any proof in YaXiang Yuan's masterpiece about Optimization \cite{WenYuSun2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-037: If x and y are vectors in $ R ^ n $ , then the inequality \begin{equation}\left| x ^T y \right| \le \left| \left| x \right| \right| _A \left| \left| y \right| \right| _{A ^{-1}}\end{equation} holds, the equality holds if and only if x and $ A ^{-1 y $ are linearly dependent, where the vector norm is the ellipsoid norm, which is deﬁned as: \begin{equation}\left| \left| v \right| \right| _A = \sqrt{ v ^T A v } .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Because A is positive deﬁnite matrix, A and identity matrix I are said to be congruent, so there is a nondegenerate square matrix $ P $ such that: $ P ^T A P = I $.
elements.sent-2411-039: Let $ B = P ^{-1 $, we have $ A = \left( P ^T \right) ^{-1 P ^{-1 = \left( P ^{-1 \right) ^T P ^{-1 = B ^T B $ .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Therefore, \begin{equation}\left| \left| x \right| \right| _A = \sqrt {x ^T A x} = \sqrt {x ^T B ^T B x} = \sqrt { \left( B x \right) ^T \left( B x \right) } = \left| \left| B x \right| \right| _2\end{equation} Because A is positive deﬁnite, A is invertible, $ A ^{-1 = B ^{-1 B ^{-T $.}}}
elements.formula-2411-015: \begin{equation}\left| \left| x \right| \right| _A = \sqrt {x ^T A x} = \sqrt {x ^T B ^T B x} = \sqrt { \left( B x \right) ^T \left( B x \right) } = \left| \left| B x \right| \right| _2\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-016: \begin{equation}\left| \left| y \right| \right| _{A ^{-1}} = \sqrt {y ^T A ^{-1} y} = \sqrt {y ^T B ^{-1} B ^{-T} y} \notag = \sqrt {\left( B ^{-T} y \right) ^T \left( B ^{-T} y \right)} = \left| \left| B ^{-T} y \right| \right| _2\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-017: \begin{equation}\left| x ^T y \right| = \left| x ^T A A ^{-1} y\right| = \left| x ^T \left( B ^T B \right) \left( B ^{-1} B ^{-T} \right) y \right| \notag = \left| \left( B x \right) ^T B B ^{-1} \left( B ^{-T} y \right) \right| = \left| \left( B x \right) ^T \left(B ^{-T} y \right) \right| \notag \le \left| \left| B x \right| \right| _2 \left| \left| B ^{-T} y \right| \right| _2\end{equation}

elements.section-2411-003: Find the steepest descent direction on the unit sphere under the ellipsoid norm
elements.sent-2411-044: They are known in the problem.
elements.sent-2411-045: We will constrain it on the unit sphere under the ellipsoid norm.
elements.sent-2411-046: It follows from Generalized Cauchy-Schwartz Inequality that: \begin{equation}\left| g _k ^T d \right| \le \left( d ^T B _k d \right) ^{\frac 1 2} \left( g _k ^T B _k ^{-1} g _k \right) ^{\frac 1 2}\end{equation} , the equality holds if and only if $ d $ and $ B _k ^{-1 g _k $ are linearly dependent.}
elements.formula-2411-019: \begin{equation}\left| g _k ^T d \right| \le \left( d ^T B _k d \right) ^{\frac 1 2} \left( g _k ^T B _k ^{-1} g _k \right) ^{\frac 1 2}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-047: Therefore, the minimization of $g _k ^T d $ subject to constraints on its variables is: \begin{equation}- \left( d ^T B _k d \right) ^{\frac 1 2} \left( g _k ^T B _k ^{-1} g _k \right) ^{\frac 1 2} = -\left( g _k ^T B _k ^{-1} g _k \right) ^{\frac 1 2}\end{equation} , which is a constant independent of decision variable $ d $.
elements.formula-2411-020: \begin{equation}- \left( d ^T B _k d \right) ^{\frac 1 2} \left( g _k ^T B _k ^{-1} g _k \right) ^{\frac 1 2} = -\left( g _k ^T B _k ^{-1} g _k \right) ^{\frac 1 2}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-049: And $ B _k $ is positive deﬁnite, so is $ B _k ^{-1 $. \begin{equation}d = r B _k ^{-1} g _k , \quad g _k ^T d = r \left( g _k ^T B _k ^{-1} g _k \right)\end{equation} .}
elements.formula-2411-021: \begin{equation}d = r B _k ^{-1} g _k , \quad g _k ^T d = r \left( g _k ^T B _k ^{-1} g _k \right)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-051: Because of $ \left| \left| d \right| \right| _{B _k =1 $, $ d $ has to be a unit vector in the direction of $ - B _k ^{-1 g _k $: \begin{equation}d = \frac {- B _k ^{-1} g _k} {\left| \left| - B _k ^{-1} g _k \right| \right| _{B _k}} = \frac {- B _k ^{-1} g _k} {\left[ \left( B _k ^{-1} g _k \right) ^T B _k \left( B _k ^{-1} g _k \right) \right] ^{\frac 1 2}} \notag =\frac {- B _k ^{-1} g _k} {\left[ g _k ^T \left( B _k ^{-1} \right) ^T B _k B _k ^{-1} g _k \right] ^{\frac 1 2}}\end{equation} $ B _k $ is symmetric positive deﬁnite, so $ \left( B _k ^{-1 \right) ^T = \left( B _k ^T \right) ^{-1 = B _k ^{-1 $, therefore \begin{equation}d = \frac {- B _k ^{-1} g _k} {\left( g _k ^T B _k ^{-1} g _k \right) ^{\frac 1 2}}\end{equation} It is the solution of the minimization problem for constrained optimization (3.1).}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-022: \begin{equation}d = \frac {- B _k ^{-1} g _k} {\left| \left| - B _k ^{-1} g _k \right| \right| _{B _k}} = \frac {- B _k ^{-1} g _k} {\left[ \left( B _k ^{-1} g _k \right) ^T B _k \left( B _k ^{-1} g _k \right) \right] ^{\frac 1 2}} \notag =\frac {- B _k ^{-1} g _k} {\left[ g _k ^T \left( B _k ^{-1} \right) ^T B _k B _k ^{-1} g _k \right] ^{\frac 1 2}}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-052: So Quasi-Newton method is a steepest descent method under the ellipsoid norm.
elements.sent-2411-053: Although Optimization software libraries in deep learning, contain a variety of quasi-Newton algorithms, most people are usually less familiar with the underlying mathematical theory.
elements.sent-2411-054: And it is seriously proved that Quasi-Newton method is a steepest descent method under the ellipsoid norm.. \bibliographystyle{elsarticle-num.}
